[IMAGE:0]Midnight is breathing down our necks and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.2 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week.  This is my sixth-fourth published book and sixteenth novel, and it's a sequel to my first novel, The Fires of Shalsha. Like that earlier novel, Journey Star is set on the second planet of Epsilon Eridani, which in the story was colonized two centuries back by a generation ship from Earth; like the earlier novel, it starts with a standard sort of conflict between seeming good and evil, but doesn't stay there. Having villains who are bad just because the heroes need someone to scorn and annihilate makes for dreary fiction; maybe, just maybe, even the most monstrous actions can be justified or even necessary in the eyes of those who do them...

And that's all I'm going to say about this strangest (so far) of my novels. All things considered, I think it worked fairly well. If you're interested, you can get copies here if you're in the United States and here if you're elsewhere.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed, and no further comments will be put through. See you next week!***

comments.title: The Astral Light?
comments.content:
So two years ago or a little less I had an experience that proved to me the existence of telepathy. I have written about this here before, but now I have some questions about it.

One day while driving to the mall I repeated aloud my heart’s desire. This desire could be expressed in as few as three words. I did this for no more than ten minutes, but with great intensity and conviction.

The very next day while walking across the parking lot of the very same mall, on my way to the grocery store, I was suddenly confronted by a man, a total stranger who stood directly in front of me, pointed right at me and said loudly: “Sir…” Then he spoke the three words, several times over: the same declarative statement about myself, only in the second person instead of in the first person.

This happened again shortly afterward, inside the store. At checkout the same man came up to me and repeated the same phrase in the same way.

Not for a millisecond do I believe that this happened by mere coincidence. Somehow my thought propagated by some immaterial means and was caught by the mind of this individual, who correctly perceived and repeated it back to me.

This suggests to me two things. First, it seems there must be some medium or field through which thoughts can propagate - especially, it seems, thoughts that generate some strong feeling. Second, it appears that our thoughts have the power to influence the minds of other persons, and hence - very importantly, I think - to influence the way they conduct themselves toward us.

So here follow my questions. First, what is the nature of this medium through which thoughts propagate from one mind to another? Does this kind of transmission of consciousness occur on the astral plane? Is it the astral light, orgone, Odic force, or something else? Or is consciousness as such the means of transmission?

Second, it seems to me that it would be most useful to be able to deliberately use this phenomenon to one’s advantage. Within the bounds of ethics, should it not be possible to affect the consciousness of others in ways that might make the difference between one’s own failure and success, between good and evil outcomes? If so, what means exactly are available to do that?

For the last few years I’ve been following the ideas of Neville Goddard and other New Thought authors. Goddard says that the world is a mirror that reflects back to you your own thoughts, feelings and assumptions; and in the case of the present incident, something like that principle does seem to have been at work. But this is my hypothesis; it’s not like I have a developed theory, with just one direct experience of this sort, albeit a to-me meaningful one.

replies.title: Re: The Astral Light?
replies.content:
First of all, welcome to the real world. It's a lot more interesting than the fantasy world that materialists think they inhabit, isn't it?

Now, for your questions:

1) All these are labels applied by different people to aspects of the same complex reality. From the point of view of traditional occult philosophy, the model I find useful, the world of matter is the densest of seven planes of existence. Orgone, odic force, eloptic energy, animal magnetism, vril, qi, prana, voor, and so on are all the second densest, which occultists call the etheric plane. The astral light belongs to the next less dense, the astral plane, which is the plane of dreams and imagination, and the most common medium through which telepathy operates.

2) Of course it's possible to do this, and there's an ancient and very thoroughly developed body of knowledge about doing so. One common name for that body of knowledge is "magic." I've written some books on what the means are and how to learn them.

3) New Thought is a good starting place. Have you read The Kybalion, by any chance? That's a useful bridge between New Thought and the older occult traditions from which New Thought took all its basic ideas.

replies.content:

Thanks for a very thorough and helpful answer. I feel much encouraged.

comments.title: Do you know my dream location?
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I just have to ask this.

A bit of background:

I had kind of a rough 2022 and 2023 for reasons we can all guess, including, I believe, being cursed by one of those natural witches who was my boss.

I was pretty isolated for about eighteen months. I wasn't very happy, but I believe I learned to control my energy during that time.

In 2024 a series of strangely miraculous events turned my life around. I feel I'm in a particularly fortunate situation now. I don't quite know what to make of it, but I try to be grateful.

I have this dream, quite often, where I go to this place that's just oneness and harmony and bliss. It's hard to describe. Sometimes people I don't know greet me like I'm a hero. Sometimes I just float by myself. I can see our current world from there but nothing about this world ever worries me while I'm there. Sometimes I tell the people there that I have to go back instead of staying with them, and I come back alone. Sometimes I just wake up and I'm back. I'm not unhappy about coming back or anything, though I'd much rather stay in the oneness and bliss. I'm always insanely happy after one of those dreams. All the plants talk to me, the rain is alive. I'm a grinning idiot for days. I'm not quite so blissed-out that I can't function, but there is a bit of that.

Do you have some highly-technical view about where it is that I go?

replies.title: Re: Do you know my dream location?
replies.content: Sure. It's someplace high up on the astral plane. When you come back, do you feel any particular creative inspiration?

replies.title: Re: Do you know my dream location?
replies.content: Thanks for that. Hmmm. Not creative exactly, but I can see a version of me who does all the things I do much more perfectly. And I can remember that I had no criticism of this world from there, so I see people, things and events as perfect expressions of soul.

replies.title: Re: Do you know my dream location?
replies.content:
Sixth region of the astral plane, then. The seventh, the highest region, is pretty reliably associated with artistic, literary, or musical creation.

The sixth region is nothing to sneeze at. Memories of time spent there, in dreams or between lives, are responsible for our ideas of heaven.

replies.content: Could you perhaps give a brief summary of each of the seven regions?

replies.title: Re: Do you know my dream location?
replies.content:
7th: source of creativity; heavily influenced by the mental plane
6th: impersonal thoughts and feelings; altruism
5th: personal thoughts and feelings; benevolence
4th: interest or indifference toward other things
3rd: personal desires, dislikes, cravings and aversions
2nd: likes, dislikes, and opinions picked up from others
1st: blind passons and hatreds; heavily influenced by the etheric plane

(These are sometimes numbered in the opposite order)

Note that the lower two regions are dark, turbid, and confused; nightmares happen here. The higher two regions are bright, clear, and insightful; healing and prophetic dreams happen here.

We do know a little about them:

5th: /

3rd: realm of functions
2nd: realm of names

replies.title: Re: Do you know my dream location?
replies.content:
Can I assume that you recognize that this is an expression of a particular gift you've been blessed with for this lifetime? Others have different gifts that, once awakened, might allow them to explore the etheric plane or the mental plane instead. It would not be at all unusual for your deep work getting out from under the curses your witch boss dumped on you to have led to this opening up of energy channels that allow your gift to begin manifesting in this way. That awakening is a significant accomplishment, and those astral beings are greeting you as a hero because it is a heroic feat for you to have discovered how to access their realm. Congratulations!

If you are interested in exploring further in the particular direction that your gift lies in, you might want to read Greg Doyle's book "Awakening the Giant Within: A Personal Adventure into the Astral Realms". Doyle's gift is almost inconceivably potent, allowing him to travel through the astral realms at will, while asleep. Alas, Doyle's gift in the creative, imaginal realm has not protected him from that realm's frequent downside of becoming lost in or disoriented by one's own imaginings. He ends up interpreting almost everything astral as being real, when so much of it is metaphorical. He also develops the kind of moral certitude and pomposity that is such a predictable side effect of being blessed with an almost superhuman gift. Nevertheless, he can go places the rest of us can't, so Doyle's writing of a travelogue to give the rest of us a taste of his adventures is a profound blessing.

— Christophe

Other people are really struggling to catch up. I   kind of do know this is a gift. I spent many years in dreams trying and trying to get higher in the astral realms. You really really don't want to get stuck down the bottom. You sort of have to lie your life as well as the other stuff, as I'm sure you know. Thanks for the interest and the kind wishes.

comments.title: Angels
comments.content:
Good morning, JMG.

I have a few questions concerning angels and other entities.

1. How does one go about formally introducing themselves to archangels and angels and developing a working relationship with them in a manner that is respectful, reciprocal, and discourages impostor spirits or 'dialing the wrong number' (eg seeking Samael as the Martian archangel but getting the qlippoth of Hod instead)? I figure that prayer and scrying (preceded by a banishing ritual and followed by meditation) are good options. Any suggestions or recommended resources, or is what I proposed sufficient?

2. I assume that the elemental and planetary archangels (eg Raphael and Cassiel) correspond to the mental plane and that angels (eg Chassan for air) and angelic choirs operate in the astral plane as reflections of the archangels' powers. What about the elemental rulers (Ariel, Cherub, Tharshis, and Seraph)? Based on the names, I assume these are angels, but where do they fit in? What can they do that the other angels can't?

3. If the Elemental Kings are the consciousness of the elements, I figure that these are astral beings in the Anima Mundi. Consequently, I see an overlap with astral-level elemental angels (eg again Chassan). Is this a case of talking about the same entities using different terminology, or are these traditionally seen as separate entities with different offices and functions? I suppose a similar question could be asked about the planetary intelligences vs the planetary angelic choirs.

4. And finally, have you ever heard of anyone successfully interacting or working with angels on a no-name basis, calling on them only by their office/function (eg Archangel of Water)? If not, this might be an interesting source of experimentation for polytheists or non-Abrahamic monotheists who like the idea of angels as messengers of the Divine yet wish to avoid the Judeo-Christian filter/interface of traditional angel lore.

As always, thank you for hosting Magic Monday!

replies.title: Re: Angels
replies.content:
1) Angels and archangels already know all about you, so it's a little absurd to try to introduce yourself to them. Remember that they are far more intelligent than we are; they also have a mental capacity we don't have, a kind of intuitive access to the truth about things. (It's one of the ironies of history that medieval philosophers called this capacity intellectus, as it's almost completely unlike what we call "intellect." Prayer and scrying are good ways to open yourself to contact with them, yes.

2 & 3) Nah, archangels belong to the spiritual plane, angels to the mental plane, elemental rulers to the astral plane, and the elemental kings (Djin, Paralda, Nichsa, and Ghob) to the etheric plane.

That's an aspect of Western presentations of Buddhism I also find distasteful and inaccurate in terms of my own experience. I've had Buddhists of considerable experience, however, inform me that the whole emptiness-and-annihilation business is what you get when you try to translate certain very subtle and complex concepts out of the cultures that created them into a culture that has no good way to talk about them.

The experience you've had is quite common among Western mystics and occultists, for what it's worth. The idea is that the personality we each have is a temporary narrowing and concentration of focus of a consciousness that, ultimately speaking, is not separate from the Infinite.

It's also well documented in Hinduism, in which one classic phrasing is Tat Tvam Asi, literally, "that also art Thou" -- whatever you look at is also you. If these make sense to you, why, there are plenty of good English translations of the principal Upanishads; you might consider reading one.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
David Chapman, a Western Tantric Buddhist (and funny enough, given your other comment below, an atheist and tech-bro-adjacent Buddhist), interprets the notion of emptiness as "nebulousity" — a lack of definition, of being definitely one thing/way or another.

The paradigmatic example he uses is that of clouds: there is no fact of the matter which molecules are part of a particular cloud and which are not, or where one cloud ends and another begins. (The term "nebulousity" was chosen specifically because it's literal meaning is "cloud-like-ness.")

But on the other side, following the Heart Sutra, he says that emptiness is inseparable from form, or in his terminology, pattern: there is no fact where a cloud begins or ends, but nevertheless there is a cloud there and insisting otherwise when you clearly see it is silly.

So, he says, everything is marked by both emptiness and form; or in other words, everything is both nebulous and patterned, because these two things are always present, though sometimes one is much more evident than the other (a rock is pretty far over on the pattern side of the scale, a cloud pretty far over on nebulosity side). One of the things Buddhism is asking you to do apply this notion to your sense of self.

This may or may not be how Asian Buddhists actually think of sunyata and rupa, but when I was looking at Buddhism seriously I found it a lot more helpful than the usual prattle about "the void" and "no self."

If you want to dig into Chapman's thought more, the best place to start is probably this page and it's successor. That site is mainly interested in talking about meaning as a nebulous/patterned phenomenon, but those pages lay out his ideas on the two concepts.

If you want the pages where he writes specifically about Buddhism, take a look at vividness.live. Chapman was a member of the Aro gTer lineage of Tibetan Buddhism — a Dzogchen lineage rather than strictly Tantric but it includes Tantra.

There's also his unfinished web novel, Buddhism for Vampires and his extended commentary on rationality (and why rationalism isn't rational), In the Cells of the Eggplant, and his critiques of modern AI, bluntly named Better Without AI. (He's an atheist and tech-bro-adjacent, but he's a dissident voice in both circles.)

Changing gears, if you want to dig into the Hindu school of Advaita Vedanta — which is similar to Buddhism but concludes that the Self is very real and is in fact the only real thing — and you do video, I recommend Swami Sarvapriyananda's lectures on Dṛg Dṛśya Viveka and Aparokshanubhuti, the two main texts of Advaita Vedanta.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Oh, one more thing: Chapman has an excellent article on the problems of mindfulness meditation, including the fact that it is a modern reinvention by a tradition whose goal is difficult to distinguish from becoming an emotionless zombies.

Are there general principles about how to navigate the inflection that the egregors of the groups that a devotional representation's artist came from might give to devotional practices? This reminds me of that discussion the other week (I can't find it now?) about how intermediate-planar human constructions like symbols and ideas can facilitate contact from higher planes, but can also distort or color that contact in ways that might go unnoticed. I suppose furries are not clearly more dysfunctional or corrupt than the mainstream culture, especially depending on what you emphasize or de-emphasize; arguably there would be more issues with Christian devotional artwork from people only a few degrees removed from the Holy Land Crusades or the Albigensian democide. And one of the benefits of diversity is highlighting things for reflection by contrast. And having a blurry line between artistic practice developed for devotional artistic subjects versus erotic ones didn't seem to slow the Greeks down much. And the divine inspires and invests its presence in what it will.

This also reminds me that I had been considering somehow acquiring an image for devotions to Whoever it is whose will is conveyed by the details of the tricksy situation around Psalm 22 and the Crucifixion (Mt 27:35,39,46; Mk 15:23,29,33; Lk 23:34; Jn 19:24) and our limited access to surviving manuscripts, with like four different parallel or simultaneous versions of what is going on with Jesus's hands and feet depending on how the broken Hebrew ka'ari yaday weraglay (Masoretic) or kaaru yaday weraglay (Dead Sea Scrolls) or ōryxan cheiras mou kai podas mou (Seuptuagint) is rendered: one where his hands and feet are literally like a lion, one where there are gouges accompanied by tiny archaeologist's surveying posts and ropes or a tiny plow indicating that some kind of furrowing or layer-breaching excavation had been performed, and so on to catch various other plausible proposals for what the text had been, or what the divine intention for how we were to interpret the situation had otherwise been. (And maybe also where all this was happening to some other crucifixion victim, and Jesus was somewhere else, only getting lots cast for his clothes and having onlookers shake their head at him and invoking the first line of the psalm, but without having anything retrospective-prophecy-fulfilling happen to his own hands and feet -- I wasn't really clear until now on the fact that Ps 22:16/17 isn't actually itself referenced by those Gospel texts which survived.) But I'm quite at a loss as to how to find someone artistically competent who could treat the artistic subject of the facially absurd situation with adequate gravity and respect. (Maybe a properly devoted Discordian?) It feels vaguely like it would be a profanation somehow if I were to attempt it myself; perhaps that is what I would need to work on? And maybe artistic competence is of only peripheral importance here? Are you supposed to only make devotional representations using tools that you carefully never use for anything potentially profane, and maybe also consecrated ones just to be sure? There's also the issue about the subject being someone being executed in a torturous way, which seems hard to have appropriate attitudes about just by itself.

(there is an inconveniently ready joke answer here, which would be, "if someone has basically a lion for their hands and feet that's a theriomorphism, so commission Kyoht -- obviously!". though that does not necessarily do any better on the "profanation" front, also I don't know them but I expect most working artists wouldn't appreciate the grief)

comments.title: OSA work

Good morning,

I've worked my way through most of the third preliminary lesson of the Order of Spiritual Alchemy, the one about guilt. To my surprise I've found that many of the things I feel guilty about are things that are actually not my fault.

After a few sessions of writing about genuine mistakes I've made, what started to come up was guilt about things like having enough money, a good apartment, living in a relatively prosperous and peaceful country- things that frequently go under the heading 'privilege'. With gratitude for Magic Monday

Greetings JMG & assembled Magic Monday community.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not sure if I have any ideas for practices per se, but I find it helpful to remember that there’s nothing wrong with value-for-value thinking, and that exchange on the basis of mutual self interest is an inherently peaceful and beneficial dynamic that often promotes good relations between those who might otherwise conflict. Money merely lubricates the process.

In high finance where money becomes more and more abstracted from concrete value, you can start to see some real problems—but the more day to day stuff is generally benign and healthy.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's quite understandable that they should feel that way, since so much of the modern economy is best described as a racket designed to maximize everyone's misery and failure for the benefit of a small number of kleptocrats who profit from the scheme. There's nothing necessarily corrupt about money -- it's simply a system of tokens, and it can be used wisely and honestly if the people involved in the exchange choose to do so -- but in this age of the world it's been horribly abused.

On the one hand, there's much to be gained by limiting the reach of money in your life. Where you can use other options -- making or doing things yourself, doing without things you don't really need, working out nonfinancial means of exchange with other people, or simply giving things to others -- it's very good to do this. Where money is inescapable, one of the great challenges of the years ahead will be to establish networks where there are hard limits to abuse that are enforced. Just as I've created a relatively pleasant space here on the internet by establishing and enforcing rules that don't permit most kinds of abusive interpersonal behavior, that can be done among groups of people with money and other forms of exchange: use it honestly, you thrive; try to abuse it, and you land on your rump on the sidewalk to the sound of a door slamming shut. comments.title: Debilitated planets

replies.title: Re: Debilitated planets

comments.title: Hinduism and Western Occultism
comments.content:
JMG,

I have recently been doing some very basic reading on Hinduism and specifically Vedanta philosophy and I am astonished by how closely it aligns with a lot of the Western occult philosophy you talk about a lot.

Everything from

a) the different kinds of material and subtle bodies and their functions
b) the nature of karma and how it functions
c) the cycle of births and rebirths and the way in which the cycle ends

And quite a few other things - except for the Sanskrit words, it's very very close. There are probably more similarities that I am not aware of.

Is this an unusual similarity between Hinduism/Vedanta and Western occultism or is it common to have this level of agreement between religious philosophies worldwide when you go deep enough?

In general I think it is very strong evidence that something is true when widely divergent accounts from different regions and historic periods agree on a phenomenen (I think you made this argument about the Old Hag experience in Monsters too).

replies.title: Re: Hinduism and Western Occultism
replies.content: There's some direct influence between the two -- a lot of Western occultists in the late 19th and early 20th centuries eagerly read translations of Hindu writings -- but that only became an option because the basic assumptions and worldviews of the two teachings were so similar from ancient times on. Thus, like you, I treat the similarities as evidence for the truth of those common teachings -- not least because Western occultism had to survive for so many centuries in an environment in which being caught thinking like a Hindu was grounds for a very painful death. If there hadn't been some very real reason to hold to those beliefs, I doubt they'd have kept springing up over and over again...

replies.title: Re: Astrology

comments.title: Paracelsus

replies.title: Re: Paracelsus
replies.content:
Paracelsus was one of the truly great figures of Renaissance occultism, and it would be very good to see his work studied more these days. The elementals received a lot of careful study and attention for centuries after his time; they are etheric in their basic structure, but many individual elementals have evolved astral bodies, and some have gone considerably further.

re you familiar with the old tale that elementals have no souls, but can gain souls by consorting (in various senses) with human beings? That's basically correct; by interacting with us, they can, by a kind of induction, pick up the trick of establishing a rudimentary mental sheath, and when this happens they become capable of reincarnation. Some end up being reborn in human bodies and joining our current of evolution; others become, in effect, elemental intelligences. Facilitating this is one of the tasks of human initiates, and some traditions, such as the Rosicrucians, have longstanding working relationships with the elemental kingdoms, in which each side helps the other. It's a complex field of lore but worth study.

replies.title: Re: Does the mental sheathe switch genders?
replies.content: The mental sheath doesn't dissolve between lives -- it's the embryonic form of the first of our permanent bodies. Oh, I know. There's a passage from Machen's "The White People" that, to me, sums up some of the worst aspects of what Christianity has become at the hands of its more clueless devotees:

"What would your feelings be, seriously, if your cat or your dog began to talk to you, and to dispute with you in human accents? You would be overwhelmed with horror. I am sure of it. And if the roses in your garden sang a weird song, you would go mad. And suppose the stones in the road began to swell and grow before your eyes, and if the pebble that you noticed at night had shot out stony blossoms in the morning? Well, these examples may give you some notion of what sin really is."

Seriously? If something in the world shows more intelligence, or beauty, or life than we expect, that's sin? There's something profoundly evil in Machen, and it's not his revels of fauns and satyrs: it's his terror and seething hatred of anything behaving in a fashion that The Right People™ Don't Approve Of.

But it's exactly his insistence that anything an inch away from Victorian rigidity is Satan incarnate that has to be discarded, of course.

The god of the Old Testament is an earthy old sod! It's purely in later Christian theology that he's refined into a plaster parody of himself.

In Shinto theology, as I understand it, every kami has four mitama or partial spirits: the aramitama or "wild spirit," the nigimitama or "gentle spirit," the sakimitama or "lucky spirit," and the kushimitama or "wondrous spirit." The kushimitama heals and accomplishes miracles, the sakimitama brings the harvest and all other good things that occur naturally, the nigimitama consoles and blesses, and the aramitama kicks you in the rump when you deserve it, or sometimes just because.
